Welcome everyone to the Accountability Communication Cadence. So of the four pillars, this is the Accountability Cadence. And so we're going to take a little bit deeper dive into the two primary methods of communication and then how we will log them and stay, and the processes behind running it from a business standpoint. All right? So the first is daily checkings. So we're going to be recommending that you primarily use Voxer at this point in time. There may be in the future other softwares that we will use in order to keep people accountable. But at the moment, it's free and it's easy to use. All right? And that way you don't have to give yourself one away. Voxer can conveys tone with voice, especially with the new customer. It's useful and important to do that so that they kind of get your vibe. Typically, you want to do that for at least the first 30 days before transitioning to more text, which it does eventually become that way. But people do prefer having basically communication. You can communicate more via voice than you can via text. The less miscommunications. All right? It's also nice because it's available from your computer. So you can type faster if you are doing type responses. And the files are stored in the cloud. So if you have pictures and check-ins and things like that, they're all stored in the cloud to that thread. All right? Now, the responsibility is the coach. You're going to want to put the client into a Monday, Wednesday, Friday, or Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday group, which you will see in the sheet. Joey's going to break down exactly how to use the tracking sheet. But you're going to put clients in one of those two categories, just like you would normally have workouts on Monday, Wednesday, Friday, or Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday. You're going to be purposely checking in with clients on Monday, Wednesday, Friday, or Tuesday, Thursday, Saturday. And that way, it decreases your per day workload and it spreads it out throughout the week. All right? Next, you're going to respond to either of those groups on those days when it is that day. Now, for that day, so Monday would be day one, or Tuesday would be day one, depending on the group, you're going to want to ask questions that are fitness related. An example of that might be like, how are your workouts going? Are you keeping your steps in? How are you doing activity-wise? Have you gone for a walk today? How was your walk today? Did you get outside? Because it was bad today. A million ways you're going to ask the same question. But it's all related to the fitness activity that is related to where they're at right now, which is on the client tracking log. So it makes it very easy. Day two is going to be your nutrition related questions. So that might be Wednesday or Thursday, depending on the group. So nutrition related question would be like, how are you doing with your food? Do you like the foods you're having right now? Are your digestion going? Is your stomach struggling with any of the new changes that we made? How are you feeling? All of those types of things would be related to the nutrition components. Day three, which would be Friday or Saturday, depending on the group, would be questions related to energy, sleep, or mood. So hey, how are your stress levels right now? How's life outside of this? You good? Is everything all right? How are you sleeping? How's your energy levels? Those are great questions to lead in, because you can build a little bit of rapport, engage the customer, and that way they continue to get value, because they know that someone cares. And so that last one is really nice, because the first two are a little bit more practitioner-based, and the third one is much more care, like, hey, how are you feeling? And people care a lot to know that other people care about that. Now, on the days that you are not messaging them back with a lengthy response or a question, excuse me, you want to always acknowledge communication. And so either like the comment or show that you receive the comments on off days, being like, awesome. Keep it up. Great job. Things like that, so that at least they feel like that you saw it. Even though if there's a scene, you still want to actually acknowledge the communication. So on the three days a week that you're responding to their email, they'll tap on message to you, or excuse me, the inbound message to you with their scale weight. On those days, you're going to have specific questions on the off days you will just basically make sure that you receive the communication or communicate that. Now, on those days, no matter what day, you want to ensure that all clients have messaged you, your wait, their wait first thing. And just remind those that did not. Very quick. They want to know that someone is watching. That is why they're paying. They're paying for someone to care. They're paying for someone to pay attention. And that is the exchange. And that is what we're paying for. And we can do this in minutes rather than in hours. Now, client-wise, they just need to send a picture of the scale. And the reason we did that is for ease and accountability. We do it on a daily basis so that they stay engaged, and they think of this first thing in the morning every day. And that will increase the likelihood that they stay healthy. We also do the picture of the scale because whenever you try and do Google Sheets and things like that, sometimes the likelihood they fill it out is lower. And then you'd also have to have an entirely separate huge thing of Google Sheets for every single customer that you'd have to track, which could be a big pain. So it also allows them to not cheat and lie on the weight that they're doing and just type in a number. And so there's various reasons that we use the scale. And you can also use different apps to date and timestamp it so you can make sure that it's a fresh number on the scale. And this way it creates a habit for them. So for those reasons, we do the picture of the scale. And we honestly don't need to know the actual weight. We just need to make sure that we know the average when they come in for the bi-weekly check-in, which is what we will cover during the bi-weekly check-in. And so yeah, and that's the responsibility that they have beyond actually just doing the thing. So for them, this is pretty easy. All they have to do is just follow the instructions and send a picture every day. It makes fulfillment for them very easy. Now, the bi-weekly check-ins, when these are standing appointments, ideally, so if it's always Wednesday at 1, then it's always Wednesday at 1, period. And you want to keep it that way because people are creatures of habit, and then it allows you to optimize your schedule as well. Spread them over the days of the week. That's my advice to you. That way, and I'll mind you, if you do the actual total hours here, if you have a full-time client load, it's 19 hours a month. So if you do 15 minutes times 75, you're more than fine to be able to do this. Excuse me. It's 19 hours over two weeks. It's 40 hours a month. So that's with a full client load. So you'll be able to knock these out fairly quickly when you have these standing appointments. So just try and keep them blocked back to back on one, two, three days a week, and it shouldn't take you that much time. Now, we're in-person, ideally, and that's because it facilitates bolt-on sales with products, et cetera. It also, from a communication standpoint, it's much more difficult to leave someone who you're seeing in-person or on a regular basis. Now, if someone is out of state or you are out of state, you can do Zoom, but I would recommend highly leveraging the fact that we are in-person. For those of you who are like, well, why would someone want to drive 35 minutes to come? It's like, well, they drive to the gym four times a week for 35 minutes there and back, so they're still saving tons of time. And this way, it's important for you to gauge how they're feeling in-person, where you can receive a lot more data just by being in the same room or something. Why? It increases engagement, retention, and ease of communication, especially when communicating the changes of programming. There's some things that you can just demonstrate and teach better in-person, with your hands and pulling the sheet over and explaining it, things like that. And one of the number issues that happens is when you tell someone to do something new, they just not along and they don't understand. And especially if you do it via text or via email, it becomes possibly difficult for somebody who's a newbie to understand this, which is one of the massive advantages of a hybrid model from a fulfillment standpoint is that we can communicate with much more efficiency to them for the one or two changes that we need. I would say maximum 20, but you should be pushing these at 15, any longer than that and it becomes a therapy session, which is not what it is. These are targeted communications around specific objectives that we're trying to accomplish with these people and provide solutions for them. Now, this is the micrometing layout looks like. The first three minutes should be greeting them while gathering stats. So it's like, hey, Sandra, hop on the scale. All right, fantastic. Let's get your body fat percentage. Great. Let's take your progress photos. Awesome. And as she's doing this, you're logging them into the client looks. You're doing, you're talking to her, she's getting the stats and you're logging them all at the same time. All right, so you're putting the weight, progress pictures, body fat percentage, very simply. And you're going to want her stats of the averages is that's actually what you're going to be logging. All right, so we're going to be tracking the other two and that's baseline just every other week, excuse me, every other meeting. You can either do the progress pictures once a month and then the body fat the other one time per month. Okay, the weight they're going to be giving to you so that you can average it out and put it in. All right, mind you, if they're always taking their scale weight with the same scale in the morning, then actually getting their weight done at the facility is not the best idea. You just want to actually get the weight from them the average of those 14 days. All right, and then you're going to do either the progress pics or the body fat percentage. If you have one of those scales like an in body, for example, where they would hop on and take their body fat percentage, that's fine, but the body fat percentage is what we're looking for. All right, now the next four minutes, we're going to be reviewing progress and giving them encouragement. So it's like, hey, look at what you've done in the last X amount of time. It's fantastic, high five, great job. How are you feeling? Okay, great. Now let's, you know, and then either address any issues they may have, and just providing encouragement that they're doing a great job, and that just the fact that they're showing up always shows that they're still motivated and they're still in it. Because ultimately, as long as you stay and you keep coming back, you're always going to eventually make progress. Like the only people who lose the ones who quit. All right, and you can quote me on that. So next four minutes, explain the new phase of fitness and nutrition that just kind of, just gives you a procedural layout of what to do from an intervention standpoint on a week to week basis. And then on a macro level every eight weeks or so, what that new phase of the plan is going to be. And so we're just going to explain to them, okay, now I want you to wear this pedometer and I want you to eat from these foods two weeks later. Now I want you to eat the same foods, just track them. Right, and then with your pedometer, I just want you to increase your stuff by 2000. That's it, right? And then all we're doing is giving these nutrition and fitness interventions, all right? After that, you're going to say, great, now we're entering this phase. This is the supplementation program that you should be using for this phase of the program. All right, and so after that, you're going to explain the consumption methods, which means you're going to just be taking these two things that are different and you don't need to take these two anymore. All right, so take this, two of these pills in the morning, right, and take this one right at dinner when you eat. That's it, all right? So just explain it to them so they understand. And that is the layout for the 15 minute bi-weekly check-in. All right, it's very targeted, very specific. The goal here is to hold them accountable, monitor stats in an easy way so they don't have to constantly sending in forms, right, that automatically update and all sorts of crap, because most of them aren't going to adhere to that over a long enough period of time. But this way, it builds the relationship, builds the trust, and then it keeps them in this cadence of twice per month. And the reason we do twice per month is because if you look at every weight loss clinic franchise, et cetera, that is typically the cadence that they've done, and I would bet dollars to donuts that they have tested, which is the best cadence to minimally be invasive to the client, but also maximally effective. All right, and so while they are coming in for this, you're going to give them according to this macro. So we just covered the micro layout, which is the last screen, and this is the macro layout. All right, and so not every week, but some weeks, there's going to be downloads that they're going to be walking out the door with. All right, and so you're going to make sure that you give them that physical copy of the thing so that they feel like there's always something that they're coming back for. People like tangible things, so give them a printout, and then you can write on it, give them notes, and then they can hand it to them. All right, and that is more or less how this works from a macro layout standpoint, and so at each one of these intervals, you're going to be, like if you're at week four, then you're going to say, hey, we're just going to keep Ultimate for another four weeks, that's it, and then we're going to downgrade you, okay? Food-wise, what I need you to do is instead of using the hand method for your foods, I want you to now actually weigh the foods, all right? It's the only thing we're changing, that's it, all right? And then on the fitness side, I want you to, instead of maintaining the steps that we did, I want you to bump it by 2,000, all right? That's it, and that is pretty much explaining everything that needs to happen from a macro standpoint to the client, and then making sure that they reciprocate and understand, and they can duplicate what you're saying and say it back to you, so like they actually get what you're talking about, all right? And so how do we actually do that? If they fail at something, give them a pain solution, one-off product, with a strategy, help them fix it. Hey, I wasn't, you know, I really was struggling to be in a moment, is because you didn't have energy, because you didn't have energy, was it because, like, to get to work, you need some pre-workout to get you going, right? Whatever it is, give them the solution, the strategy, and the product, all right? And once you transition phases of the program, you want to direct them and do not ask, right? You don't go to your oncologist and say, you know, or he doesn't ask you, or she doesn't ask you. Well, how many milligrams of this chemo medicine do you think we should take? You're like, I don't know, like, right, that's why I'm coming to you. You were the expert, they have no idea. You were the ones who has had far more experience than they do, it's going to need to direct them. This is what we're going to do, all right? And then after the hit, you get the confirmation of that.
